title: Risk Management System — Hazard Log Update SLA (Art. 9/Annex IV)
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the EU AI Act programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with EU AI Act control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for Art.13.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that EU AI Act Art.13 (Transparency and information) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.21.20: Risk Management System — Hazard Log Update SLA (Art. 9/Annex IV).
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.21.20' running on index=ai_risk sourcetype=rms:hazard — hazard_id, status, transition, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Risk Management System — Hazard Log Update SLA (Art. 9/Annex' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper EU AI Act control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the EU AI Act control objective.

dataSources: `index=ai_risk` `sourcetype=rms:hazard` — hazard_id, status, transition — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
index=ai_risk sourcetype="rms:hazard" earliest=-90d
| eval opened=if(status="new",_time,null())
| eval reviewed=if(status="reviewed",_time,null())
| stats min(opened) as t_open, max(reviewed) as t_rev by hazard_id
| eval review_hours=round((t_rev-t_open)/3600,2)
| where isnull(t_rev) OR review_hours>168
| sort - review_hours
description: Measures time from new_hazard to hazard_reviewed in risk register stream for RMS operational effectiveness.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for EU AI Act compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes ai_risk and sourcetypes rms:hazard, rms:hazard`. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Ensure hazard_id stable; (2) escalate unreviewed >7d; (3) link to incident index; (4) quarterly RMS committee export. Indexes required: `index=ai_risk`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=rms:hazard`, `sourcetype=rms:hazard``.
visualization: Histogram (review_hours), Table (open hazards), Single value (mean review_hours)
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `ai_risk` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.13`. The control objective is: "Evidence that EU AI Act Art.13 (Transparency and information) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.21.20: Risk Management System — Hazard Log Update SLA (Art. 9/Annex IV)." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `ai_risk` under `rms:hazard`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Ensure hazard_id stable. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** escalate unreviewed >7d. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** link to incident index. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** quarterly RMS committee export. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

The detection fires when `review_hours > 168`. For "Risk Management System — Hazard Log Update SLA (Art. 9/Annex IV)," the aggregation exposes change activity — change frequency, change authorship, and change surface are the dimensions the regulation cares about, not raw events. This threshold is the operational heart of the control — set it too low and the signal drowns in noise; set it too high and real compliance deviations go undetected. The value `168` should never be treated as universal; it must be calibrated against your specific operational baseline.

Calibration procedure: before enabling this as a production alert, run the base search over the last 30 days with the threshold removed and compute distribution statistics:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats count by review_hours
| stats avg(review_hours) as mean, perc50(review_hours) as p50, perc90(review_hours) as p90, perc99(review_hours) as p99, max(review_hours) as hi
```

Set the production threshold between p90 and p99 depending on your organisation's alert fatigue tolerance. Document the chosen value in the control specification alongside the date of calibration and the sample size — auditors will ask how you arrived at `168` and the defensible answer is 'data, plus a documented review by the control owner,' not 'the template said so.'

The `min()` aggregation over the last 90 days is a deliberate choice. Compliance reports on sustained behaviour, not instantaneous spikes — a single noisy event crossing the threshold for one second should not page the on-call. If your organisation requires point-in-time sensitivity (e.g. real-time fraud detection), shorten the time window and aggregation span; for accountability reporting, widen them. Tune for the regulation's actual expectation, not engineering preference.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.21.20: Risk Management System — Hazard Log Update SLA (Art. 9/Annex]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -90d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.21.20,reg=EU_AI_Act
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=ai_risk sourcetype=rms:hazard earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `hazard_id`, `opened`, `review_hours`, `reviewed`, `t_open`, `t_rev`. Run:

```spl
index=ai_risk sourcetype=rms:hazard earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table hazard_id, opened, review_hours, reviewed, t_open, t_rev
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `review_hours`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(review_hours) as p50 perc90(review_hours) as p90 perc99(review_hours) as p99 max(review_hours) as hi
```

The current threshold `168` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.21.20: EU AI Act evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Histogram (`review_hours`):** Reveals the distribution shape of the measured metric. Useful for calibrating thresholds — the shape tells you whether the current threshold sits on the tail or in the body of the distribution.

**Panel 2 — Drill-through table (`open hazards`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`mean review_hours`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'EU AI Act current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Too many or too few alerts:** The threshold is almost certainly miscalibrated for your estate — re-run the percentile calibration (Step 3d) on a representative 30-day window and pick a value between p90 and p99.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**EU AI Act-specific failure modes:**

• Risk category sensitivity: AI Act defines Prohibited / High / Limited / Minimal risk. Detections must fire only on systems classified High or above — Minimal-risk systems like spam filters are out of scope and noise from them hides real findings.

• General-Purpose AI (GPAI) disclosure: foundation models have specific transparency obligations. Detections on model provenance must differentiate GPAI from task-specific models; GPAI adds requirements that task-specific models don't trigger.

• Post-market monitoring (Art. 72): High-risk AI systems require post-market monitoring plans. Detections are evidence of such monitoring — align the detection's measurement cadence and scope with the system's PMMS document.

• Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment: FRIA is required for many public-sector deployments. Detections on fairness/bias metrics must be reconcilable to the FRIA's specific risk indicators, not generic AI fairness metrics.
grandmaExplanation: We measure time from new_hazard to hazard_reviewed in risk register stream for RMS operational effectiveness.
